risks.title: Infrastructure strain
risks.description: The rapid increase in housing supply could outpace infrastructure development, leading to congestion and service bottlenecks. This impacts a wide range of demographics across various cities, from renters in Toronto to owners in Edmonton and PEI Rural.
risks.affected_groups: Renters and owners across urban and rural areas
risks.cascade_effect: Further infrastructure strain could lead to higher taxes or reduced public services, compounding affordability challenges.

risks.title: Timeline-driven substandard housing
risks.description: The rapid construction timeline may result in substandard housing, impacting long-term value and safety standards. This risk is amplified due to potential substandard quality affecting both northern and urban communities.
risks.affected_groups: Owners and renters in Northern Ontario, Kelowna, and Hamilton
risks.cascade_effect: Substandard housing quality could increase maintenance costs and decrease property values, straining finances.

risks.title: Affordability crisis
risks.description: The increased housing supply may paradoxically drive up rental prices in certain areas while reducing property values, adversely affecting both low-income renters and senior homeowners.
risks.affected_groups: Low-income renters and senior homeowners in Kelowna, Sudbury, and Montreal
risks.cascade_effect: Increased rental prices could lead to higher property taxes, reducing affordability further.

risks.title: Geographic disparity
risks.description: The policy's urban focus may worsen the shortage of affordable housing in rural and remote areas, widening the geographic disparity. This could impact renters and owners in Northern Ontario and Nunavut.
risks.affected_groups: 50-64 owners and 25-34 renters in rural and remote areas
risks.cascade_effect: This disparity could lead to increased regional migration pressures, further straining urban resources.

risks.title: Increased fiscal burden
risks.description: Potential increase in property taxes and municipal fees could strain the budgets of various demographics, including retired fixed-income and high-income groups.
risks.affected_groups: Established immigrants and retired low-income homeowners in cities like Edmonton and Montreal
risks.cascade_effect: Higher taxes reduce disposable income further compounding affordability issues.

risk_report.blind_spots: The impacts on Indigenous communities and newcomers are underrepresented.
risk_report.key_insight: Rapid construction timelines are leading to downstream risks across multiple sectors, especially infrastructure and housing quality.

confidence.reason: strong StatsCan data coverage for this policy type; all agents responded successfully
confidence.caveat: This is a hypothesis generation tool. Findings should be validated against real survey data before informing policy decisions.
